message:
Review and improve plan beads: acceptance criteria and implementation notes

- 55 beads reviewed, 9 improved (5 P3 standalones got acceptance criteria,
  3 methodology beads got implementation notes, 1 label fix)
- Zero dependency cycles

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-13i9","title":"Implement FUSE statfs, fallocate, link, and symlink operations","description":"# Implement FUSE statfs, fallocate, link, and symlink operations

## GOAL
Implement the remaining FUSE operations needed for full filesystem functionality.

## OPERATIONS

### 1. statfs - Filesystem statistics
```rust
fn statfs(&self, ino: u64, reply: fuser::ReplyStatfs) {
    // Return filesystem statistics:
    // - blocks: total blocks
    // - bfree: free blocks
    // - bavail: available blocks (to non-root)
    // - files: total inodes
    // - ffree: free inodes
    // - bsize: block size
    // - namelen: max filename length
    // - frsize: fragment size
    
    let sb = self.fs.superblock();
    reply.statfs(
        sb.blocks_count,
        sb.free_blocks_count,
        sb.free_blocks_count, // available = free for now
        sb.inodes_count,
        sb.free_inodes_count,
        sb.block_size,
        255, // max name length
        sb.block_size,
    );
}
```

### 2. fallocate - Preallocate space
```rust
fn fallocate(
    &self, 
    ino: u64, 
    fh: u64, 
    offset: i64, 
    length: i64, 
    mode: i32, 
    reply: fuser::ReplyEmpty
) {
    // Modes:
    // - 0: allocate space
    // - FALLOC_FL_KEEP_SIZE: allocate but don't change size
    // - FALLOC_FL_PUNCH_HOLE: deallocate (with KEEP_SIZE)
    
    // 1. Validate mode
    // 2. Calculate block range
    // 3. Allocate/deallocate blocks via extent tree
    // 4. Update inode if needed
}
```

### 3. link - Create hard link
```rust
fn link(
    &self,
    ino: u64,
    newparent: u64,
    newname: &OsStr,
    reply: fuser::ReplyEntry,
) {
    // 1. Validate source inode exists
    // 2. Check link count limit (65000 for ext4)
    // 3. Add directory entry in newparent
    // 4. Increment link count on source inode
    // 5. Return entry attributes
}
```

### 4. symlink - Create symbolic link
```rust
fn symlink(
    &self,
    parent: u64,
    name: &OsStr,
    link: &Path,
    reply: fuser::ReplyEntry,
) {
    // 1. Allocate inode with S_IFLNK mode
    // 2. Store link target:
    //    - inline if len <= 60 bytes (ext4_inode.i_block)
    //    - in data block if longer
    // 3. Add directory entry
    // 4. Return entry attributes
}
```

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit: statfs returns correct values
2. Unit: fallocate allocates blocks without writing data
3. Unit: fallocate punch_hole removes blocks
4. Unit: link increments link count
5. Unit: link fails at limit (EMLINK)
6. Unit: symlink with short target stores inline
7. Unit: symlink with long target uses block
8. Integration: df shows correct space
9. Integration: hardlinks share data

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- statfs (trace): returned values
- fallocate (debug): inode, offset, length, mode, blocks allocated
- link (debug): source ino, parent, name, new link count
- symlink (debug): parent, name, target, storage mode

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. df command shows correct filesystem usage
2. fallocate preallocates without writing zeros
3. Hard links share underlying data
4. Symlinks resolve correctly
5. All ops return correct errno on error","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:16:03.821187089Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T03:56:38.079705536Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["core","ext4","fuse"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-13i9","depends_on_id":"bd-2ad","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:52:34.187931677Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-13i9","depends_on_id":"bd-2s4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:56:38.079636026Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-13i9","depends_on_id":"bd-huh.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:16:10.642267335Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-14c","title":"Add performance regression test with baseline tracking","description":"# Add performance regression test with baseline tracking

## GOAL
Implement automated performance regression detection so no commit silently degrades performance.

## BACKGROUND
From AGENTS.md:
- Benchmark loop is mandatory
- Baseline first, then optimize
- Re-measure and record deltas

We need automated CI integration.

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Baseline Storage
```
benchmarks/
├── baselines/
│   ├── latest.json           # Current baseline
│   └── history/
│       ├── 2026-02-12.json
│       └── ...
├── results/
│   └── <commit-sha>.json
└── thresholds.toml           # Regression thresholds
```

### 2. Benchmark Suite
```rust
// In ffs-harness
criterion_group!(
    perf_regression,
    bench_read_throughput,
    bench_write_throughput,
    bench_lookup_latency,
    bench_readdir_latency,
    bench_mvcc_snapshot,
    bench_mvcc_commit,
    bench_arc_hit,
    bench_arc_miss,
);
```

### 3. Regression Detection
```toml
# thresholds.toml
[thresholds]
read_throughput = { min_ratio = 0.9 }  # max 10% regression
write_throughput = { min_ratio = 0.85 }
lookup_latency = { max_ratio = 1.1 }   # max 10% regression
```

### 4. CI Integration
```yaml
# .github/workflows/perf.yml
- name: Run benchmarks
  run: cargo bench -p ffs-harness -- --save-baseline current

- name: Check regression
  run: scripts/check_perf_regression.sh current baselines/latest.json
```

### 5. Reporting
- JSON output with all metrics
- Human-readable summary
- PR comment with delta table

## TESTS
1. Unit: Threshold checking logic
2. Integration: Benchmark suite runs to completion
3. Integration: Regression detected when introduced

## LOGGING
- Benchmark results (info): metric, value, baseline, ratio
- Regression (warn): metric, threshold exceeded
- Pass (info): all within threshold

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. CI fails on performance regression beyond threshold
2. Baselines updated on main branch
3. History preserved for trend analysis
4. Clear reporting of which metric regressed","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T02:54:00.578131962Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T03:56:40.091917730Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","harness","perf"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-14c","depends_on_id":"bd-3ib","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:56:40.091863809Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-14c","depends_on_id":"bd-3ib.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:53:29.732136146Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-14jb","title":"EPIC: EBR for Block Version GC (Graveyard Entry 14.10)","description":"# EPIC: EBR for Block Version GC (Graveyard Entry 14.10)

## PURPOSE
Implement Epoch-Based Reclamation (EBR) for block version chain garbage collection. Without GC, MVCC version chains grow unbounded — every committed write creates a new version that is never freed. EBR provides safe, lock-free reclamation of old versions once no active transaction can observe them.

## BACKGROUND
From the Alien CS Graveyard entry 14.10:
- EBR: threads announce entry into epochs; old block versions freed after all threads advance past the epoch in which versions were retired
- Critical for MVCC scalability — without GC, memory usage is O(total_writes) instead of O(active_versions)
- crossbeam-epoch provides production-quality EBR in Rust

## DESIGN

### Epoch Lifecycle
1. Each MVCC reader pins the current epoch on transaction begin
2. Writers create new versions and retire old versions (mark for deferred free)
3. Background GC thread advances the global epoch periodically
4. Retired versions are freed when all threads have advanced past the retire epoch

### Version Chain Integration
- Version chain: Block -> V3 -> V2 -> V1 (newest first)
- Active snapshot with commit_seq=5 may still need V2
- V1 can be freed only when no active snapshot has commit_seq <= V1.commit_seq
- EBR epoch pins ensure no version is freed while observable

### crossbeam-epoch Integration
- Use crossbeam::epoch::{pin, Guard, Atomic, Owned}
- Version chain nodes wrapped in Atomic<VersionNode>
- On retire: guard.defer_destroy(old_version)
- GC happens automatically when guard is dropped and epoch advances

### Memory Bounds
- Version chain length bound: configurable max versions per block (e.g., 64)
- If chain exceeds bound: force-advance oldest snapshot or abort oldest transaction
- This prevents unbounded memory growth even with long-running snapshots

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Old block versions are freed after all observing transactions complete
- [ ] No use-after-free (verified with Miri or AddressSanitizer)
- [ ] Memory usage bounded: version chain length enforced
- [ ] crossbeam-epoch correctly integrated
- [ ] Benchmark: memory usage stable under continuous write load
- [ ] Unit tests: version freed after last observer drops
- [ ] Unit tests: version NOT freed while observer holds pin","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:22:59.588504156Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:39.665192956Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ebr","gc","mvcc"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-14jb","depends_on_id":"bd-22w","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:39.665139266Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-14jb.1","title":"Integrate crossbeam-epoch for block version GC","description":"# Integrate crossbeam-epoch for block version GC

## GOAL
Wire crossbeam-epoch into the MVCC version chain so that old block versions are automatically reclaimed when no active transaction can observe them.

## DESIGN

### crossbeam-epoch API Usage
- On transaction begin: let guard = crossbeam::epoch::pin()
- Version chain nodes: Atomic<VersionNode> for lock-free traversal
- On version retire: guard.defer_destroy(old_node)
- Guard dropped on transaction commit/abort -> epoch advances

### Key Changes
- ffs-mvcc/src/version_chain.rs: replace Box<VersionNode> with crossbeam Atomic
- ffs-mvcc/src/txn.rs: pin epoch on begin, drop guard on commit/abort
- Add crossbeam-epoch dependency to ffs-mvcc/Cargo.toml

### Safety
- All version chain access must go through pinned guard
- No raw pointer dereference without guard
- Miri-compatible (crossbeam-epoch works with Miri)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] crossbeam-epoch integrated into version chain
- [ ] Old versions freed after epoch advances
- [ ] No memory leaks under continuous write workload (track with allocator stats)
- [ ] Passes Miri (no undefined behavior)
- [ ] Unit test: create versions, advance epoch, verify old versions freed","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:23:09.753179032Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:22.084130922Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ebr","gc","mvcc"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-14jb.1","depends_on_id":"bd-14jb","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:23:09.753179032Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-14jb.1","depends_on_id":"bd-22w.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:22.084079816Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-14jb.2","title":"Implement version chain length bounds","description":"# Implement version chain length bounds

## GOAL
Enforce a configurable maximum version chain length per block to prevent unbounded memory growth, even when long-running snapshots pin old versions.

## DESIGN
- Max chain length: configurable, default 64 versions per block
- When chain exceeds max: emit warning, force-advance oldest snapshot epoch
- If force-advance insufficient: abort oldest read-only transaction holding the pin
- Last resort: refuse new writes to that block until GC catches up (backpressure)
- Track chain lengths in BlockVersionStats for monitoring

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Chain length bounded at configured maximum
- [ ] Long-running snapshots warned and force-advanced when blocking GC
- [ ] Backpressure applied when chain length critical
- [ ] Unit test: write 100 versions, verify chain length stays <= max","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:23:16.828361220Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:22.877574682Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["bounds","gc","mvcc"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-14jb.2","depends_on_id":"bd-14jb","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:23:16.828361220Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-14jb.2","depends_on_id":"bd-14jb.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:22.877532013Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-14jb.3","title":"Benchmark: memory usage under concurrent writers with EBR GC","description":"# Benchmark: memory usage under concurrent writers with EBR GC

## GOAL
Prove that EBR-based garbage collection keeps memory usage bounded under continuous concurrent write workloads. Without this benchmark, we cannot verify that the GC reclaims versions fast enough to prevent unbounded memory growth.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard entry 14.10: EBR (Epoch-Based Reclamation) defers freeing old block versions until all observing transactions complete. If GC lags behind writers, memory grows without bound. This benchmark measures the steady-state memory profile under realistic concurrent write loads.

## BENCHMARK SCENARIOS

### Scenario 1: Single Writer Steady State
- 1 writer thread, continuous block writes (100K transactions)
- Measure: peak RSS, version chain lengths, GC reclaim rate
- Expected: RSS stabilizes after initial ramp-up (bounded growth)

### Scenario 2: Multi-Writer Scaling (2, 4, 8, 16 threads)
- Each thread writes to random blocks, 10K transactions each
- Measure: peak RSS per thread count, GC throughput, epoch advance rate
- Expected: RSS scales sub-linearly with thread count (shared versions reclaimed)

### Scenario 3: Long-Running Reader Pinning
- 1 long-running read transaction (holds epoch pin for 30 seconds)
- 8 concurrent writers producing new versions
- Measure: RSS growth during pinned period, RSS drop after reader completes
- Expected: RSS grows during pin (versions retained), drops after unpin (GC catches up)

### Scenario 4: Hot-Key Contention
- 16 threads all writing to same 100 blocks (maximum version chain churn)
- Measure: peak chain length, GC latency, abort rate
- Expected: chain length bounded by configured max (bd-14jb.2), GC keeps up

### Scenario 5: Bursty Write Pattern
- Alternating 5s burst (max writes) and 5s idle, repeated 6 times
- Measure: RSS during burst vs idle, GC catch-up time
- Expected: RSS drops to near-baseline during idle periods

## METRICS
- Peak RSS (resident set size) via /proc/self/statm
- Version chain length histogram (p50/p95/p99/max)
- GC reclaim rate (versions freed per second)
- Epoch advance frequency
- Time to steady-state RSS

## REPORTING
- JSON output with all metrics per scenario
- Comparison table: with-EBR vs without-GC (leak baseline)
- Store results as CI artifacts for regression tracking

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] All 5 scenarios execute and produce metrics
- [ ] Single-writer RSS stabilizes (no unbounded growth)
- [ ] Multi-writer RSS scales sub-linearly with thread count
- [ ] Long-running reader: RSS recovers after reader drops pin
- [ ] Hot-key: chain length stays within configured bounds
- [ ] Bursty: RSS returns to near-baseline during idle periods
- [ ] Results stored as JSON CI artifacts
- [ ] Benchmark harness is deterministic (seeded PRNG for block selection)
- [ ] Logging: tracing spans for each scenario with memory snapshots at 1s intervals","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:23:25.662332707Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:20:20.887655649Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["benchmark","gc","mvcc"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-14jb.3","depends_on_id":"bd-14jb","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:23:25.662332707Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-14jb.3","depends_on_id":"bd-14jb.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:23.811293912Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-14jb.4","title":"E2E test: EBR GC correctness under concurrent MVCC transactions","description":"# E2E test: EBR GC correctness under concurrent MVCC transactions

## GOAL
Prove that EBR-based garbage collection correctly reclaims old block versions without ever freeing a version that an active transaction still observes. This is the safety test for the entire EBR epic.

## TEST SCENARIOS

### Scenario 1: Basic Reclamation Correctness
1. Start transaction T1 (snapshot at commit_seq=5)
2. Writer W1 creates versions V6, V7, V8 for the same block
3. V5 (observed by T1) must NOT be freed
4. T1 commits -> V5 becomes eligible for GC
5. Advance epoch -> V5 freed
6. Verify: T1 always saw V5 during its lifetime, V5 freed after T1 ended

### Scenario 2: Multi-Reader Multi-Writer
1. 4 reader transactions at different snapshots (commit_seq 10, 20, 30, 40)
2. 4 writer threads producing new versions continuously
3. Each reader reads its snapshot blocks, verifies content matches expected
4. Readers commit in reverse order (40 first, 10 last)
5. After each reader commit: verify newly eligible versions freed
6. After all readers commit: verify all obsolete versions freed

### Scenario 3: No Use-After-Free
1. Spawn reader that pins epoch, reads version V_old, sleeps 500ms, reads again
2. Writer creates new version V_new for same block
3. GC thread attempts to free V_old
4. Verify: V_old NOT freed while reader holds pin (epoch prevents reclamation)
5. Reader drops pin -> V_old freed on next epoch advance
6. Run under AddressSanitizer / Miri for UAF detection

### Scenario 4: Chain Length Enforcement
1. Create block with max_chain_length=16
2. Write 32 versions without any reader advancing
3. Verify: chain length stays at 16 (oldest versions force-freed or writer blocked)
4. Verify: no data corruption from forced chain truncation

### Scenario 5: Crash Recovery of GC State
1. Build up version chains, partially GC
2. Simulate crash (kill process)
3. Remount: version chains consistent, no dangling pointers
4. GC resumes correctly from recovered state

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] No use-after-free detected (AddressSanitizer or Miri clean)
- [ ] Readers always see consistent snapshots (no torn reads)
- [ ] Versions freed promptly after last observer completes
- [ ] Chain length bounds enforced under all scenarios
- [ ] Crash recovery preserves GC invariants
- [ ] All scenarios deterministic with seeded PRNG
- [ ] Logging: tracing spans for epoch advances, version frees, pin/unpin events","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:22:12.233542839Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:22:12.233542839Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","ebr","gc","test"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-14jb.4","depends_on_id":"bd-14jb","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:22:12.233542839Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-14jb.4","depends_on_id":"bd-14jb.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:22:12.233542839Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-22w","title":"EPIC: MVCC Core Completion","description":"# EPIC: MVCC Core Completion

## PURPOSE
Complete the MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) subsystem to production quality. Currently at 28.6% (4/14).

## BACKGROUND
MVCC is FrankenFS's key architectural innovation over kernel ext4/btrfs. It enables:
- Concurrent readers + writers without JBD2's global lock
- Snapshot isolation for consistent reads
- First-committer-wins (FCW) and SSI conflict detection

From FEATURE_PARITY.md:
- MVCC snapshot visibility: ✅
- MVCC commit sequencing: ✅
- FCW conflict detection: ✅
- Version retention policy: ✅ (in-memory)
- COW block rewrite path: 🟡 PARTIAL (basic version copy only)

## GAPS TO CLOSE
1. MVCC persistence - version chains survive restart (WAL)
2. Version chain compression - reduce memory overhead
3. GC optimization - efficient pruning under active snapshots
4. COW block rewrite path - complete write-side semantics

## ALIEN-ARTIFACT QUALITY BAR (from AGENTS.md)
MVCC conflict logic is explicitly called out for principled treatment:
- explicit invariants
- evidence ledger for commit/reject decisions
- formal SSI cycle detection (or equivalent correctness argument + tests)

## CURRENT STATE
- `MvccStore` with in-memory version chains
- Transaction APIs (`begin` / `commit` / `commit_ssi`)
- Snapshot visibility via search
- GC via `prune_versions_older_than` and watermarking

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. MVCC persistence layer (WAL or equivalent)
2. Version chain memory bounded (compression and/or GC policy)
3. Memory overhead documented and benchmarked
4. Conflict detection formally verified (proptest + deterministic interleavings)
5. MVCC/COW reaches 70%+ parity (10/14)

## DEPENDENCIES
- EPIC: Conformance & Quality Infrastructure (bd-2jk) - need baselines and goldens for safe iteration

## RELATED SPEC SECTIONS
- COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENFS_V1.md §3 (MVCC)
- PROPOSED_ARCHITECTURE.md (ffs-mvcc crate)

## Success Criteria
1. Crash-restart tests demonstrate committed versions persist and uncommitted versions do not.
2. Deterministic concurrency tests (lab runtime) cover snapshot stability and FCW/SSI invariants.
3.  {"id":"bd-22w.1","title":"Implement MVCC version persistence layer","description":"# Implement MVCC version persistence layer

## GOAL
Persist MVCC version chains so **committed** versions survive process restart (crash/restart durability for the MVCC layer).

## CURRENT REALITY (CODE)
- `crates/ffs-mvcc/src/lib.rs` contains an in-memory `MvccStore` with:
  - `Transaction { writes, reads }`
  - `commit()` and `commit_ssi()`
  - version chains stored as `BTreeMap<BlockNumber, Vec<BlockVersion>>`

On restart, all committed version history is lost.

## DESIGN OPTIONS
### Option A: Append-only WAL (recommended)
- Write-ahead log of MVCC commits.
- On startup: replay WAL into `MvccStore`.
- Periodic checkpoint compacts WAL into a base snapshot.

### Option B: Separate version-store file + index
- Append-only log + periodic index rebuild.

### Option C: Inline inside filesystem image
- Too invasive for V1.

## RECOMMENDED: WAL (Option A)
### WAL invariants
- Replay is idempotent.
- Partial/truncated tail entries are detected and ignored safely.
- Commit boundary is explicit.
- Every on-disk record is checksummed (crc32c or blake3) so corruption is detectable.

### I/O authority
All filesystem I/O must be `&asupersync::Cx`-aware (AGENTS.md: no ambient authority).

## DELIVERABLES
1. A persistence module for ffs-mvcc (new files are OK):
   - `crates/ffs-mvcc/src/wal.rs` (binary format + encode/decode)
   - `crates/ffs-mvcc/src/persist.rs` (integration glue)
2. A persistent constructor and integration points:
   - `MvccStore::open_persistent(cx, wal_path, options)` (or a wrapper type that owns `MvccStore`)
   - On `commit`/`commit_ssi`, append commit records.
3. Checkpointing:
   - a compacted snapshot format
   - atomic replacement (temp file + rename)

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Crash-restart:
   - commit some writes
   - drop store
   - reopen from WAL
   - verify latest committed versions are present
2. Uncommitted not restored:
   - write without commit, drop
   - reopen
   - verify no uncommitted versions exist
3. Truncation handling:
   - corrupt/truncate WAL tail
   - replay stops safely and does not panic

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. WAL is created and replay works deterministically.
2. Committed versions persist; uncommitted do not.
3. Checkpointing reduces WAL growth and is crash-safe.
4. All behavior is covered by tests with clear logging on failure.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T15:00:26.082439996Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T22:14:46.439100060Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T22:14:46.439001755Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["io","mvcc","semantics"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-22w.1","depends_on_id":"bd-22w","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-12T15:00:26.082439996Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-22w.2","title":"Implement version chain compression","description":"# Implement version chain compression

## GOAL
Reduce memory overhead of MVCC version chains without breaking snapshot visibility semantics.

## CURRENT REALITY (CODE)
- `MvccStore` stores full `Vec<u8>` for every `BlockVersion`.
- Hot blocks can accumulate long chains (high memory overhead).

## STRATEGIES (PHASED)
1. **Dedup identical versions** (low-risk, high-win)
   - if a staged write produces bytes identical to the latest visible version, store a reference instead of duplicating
2. **Safe chain compaction / capping**
   - prune versions older than the oldest active snapshot (already partially supported via `active_snapshots` + `prune_versions_older_than`)
   - add policy knobs for max chain length
3. **Delta encoding** (higher complexity)
   - store small diffs against a base version
   - ensure reconstruction is bounded and fast

## DELIVERABLES
1. Define a compressed representation for block version data (new module recommended):
   - `crates/ffs-mvcc/src/compression.rs`
2. Integrate into `MvccStore` so callers can still `read_visible`/`read_latest` semantics (whatever the existing read API is or will be).
3. Add microbenchmarks (optional but recommended) and documentation of tradeoffs.

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Reconstruction correctness:
   - for a chain of edits, reconstruct each version and assert byte-equality
2. Snapshot safety:
   - ensure compaction never removes a version needed by any registered active snapshot
3. Performance sanity:
   - reconstruction time is bounded for typical chain lengths (add a unit test with a reasonable upper bound)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Demonstrable memory reduction on a representative "hot block" workload.
2. No observable behavior changes (goldens + MVCC invariants).
3. Compression behavior is configurable and documented.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"GoldenLark","created_at":"2026-02-12T15:00:45.109316421Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:26:26.369116408Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mvcc","perf","semantics"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-22w.2","depends_on_id":"bd-22w","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-12T15:00:45.109316421Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-22w.2","depends_on_id":"bd-22w.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:54:39.090825206Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-22w.3","title":"Wire MVCC version store into block read/write path","description":"# Wire MVCC version store into block read/write path

## GOAL
Connect the in-memory MvccStore to actual block I/O so that:
1. Read operations see snapshot-consistent block versions
2. Write operations stage new block versions through MVCC transactions
3. The ARC cache interacts correctly with versioned blocks

## BACKGROUND
Currently, MvccStore works in isolation (tested with synthetic data). The read path in ffs-core bypasses MVCC entirely, reading raw blocks from the device. This bead connects the two.

From the write path in README:
```
ffs-core: begin write transaction
  → ffs-mvcc: create new block versions (COW)
    → ffs-block: write through cache
  → ffs-mvcc: commit (SSI validation)
```

## DESIGN
```rust
/// MVCC-aware block device wrapper
pub struct MvccBlockDevice<D: BlockDevice> {
    inner: D,
    store: Arc<MvccStore>,
}

impl<D: BlockDevice> MvccBlockDevice<D> {
    pub fn read(&self, block: BlockNumber, snapshot: Snapshot, cx: &Cx) -> Result<BlockBuf> {
        // 1. Check MvccStore for version visible to snapshot
        // 2. If found, return versioned data
        // 3. If not, fall back to inner device
    }
    
    pub fn write(&self, block: BlockNumber, data: &[u8], txn: &mut Transaction, cx: &Cx) -> Result<()> {
        // 1. Stage new version in transaction write-set
        // 2. Don't write to device yet (COW)
    }
}
```

## DELIVERABLES
1. `MvccBlockDevice<D: BlockDevice>` struct in ffs-mvcc or ffs-core
2. Transaction wrapper with begin/commit/abort lifecycle
3. Integration into OpenFs read path (snapshot reads)
4. Cache coherence: dirty blocks from MVCC go through cache write-back
5. Tests proving snapshot isolation with real block I/O

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit: Two concurrent readers see consistent snapshots
2. Unit: Writer creates new version, reader on old snapshot doesn't see it
3. Unit: FCW conflict correctly aborts second committer
4. Unit: Committed writes visible to subsequent transactions
5. Unit: Abort discards staged writes
6. Integration: Read-modify-write cycle with snapshot isolation
7. Integration: ARC cache hit for versioned block
8. Benchmark: No performance regression >5% on read path

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
### Read path logging
- mvcc_read_start (trace): block, snapshot
- mvcc_version_hit (trace): block, version_commit_seq
- mvcc_version_miss (trace): block, snapshot (falling back to device)
- mvcc_read_complete (trace): block, source (mvcc/device), duration_us

### Write path logging
- mvcc_write_stage (trace): block, txn_id, data_len
- mvcc_write_conflict (debug): block, txn_id, conflicting_commit_seq

### Transaction logging
- txn_begin (debug): txn_id, snapshot_seq
- txn_commit_start (debug): txn_id, write_set_size, read_set_size
- txn_commit_success (info): txn_id, commit_seq, duration_us
- txn_commit_conflict (warn): txn_id, conflict_type (fcw/ssi), conflicting_block
- txn_abort (debug): txn_id, reason

### Cache coherence logging
- cache_invalidate (trace): block, reason (mvcc_commit)
- cache_writeback (trace): block, version_commit_seq

### Performance logging
- slow_txn_commit (info): txn_id, duration_ms (if > 100ms)
- large_write_set (debug): txn_id, write_set_size (if > 1000)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. All existing read-path tests still pass
2. MVCC snapshot isolation verified with block-level I/O
3. No performance regression >5% on read path
4. Cache coherence maintained
5. Logging sufficient for debugging isolation violations","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:51:10.376191065Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T06:00:32.093979897Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T06:00:32.093947216Z","close_reason":"Implemented MVCC block device wiring into OpenFs:

## Deliverables Completed:
1. ✅ Added Arc<RwLock<MvccStore>> field to OpenFs struct
2. ✅ Transaction lifecycle methods: begin_transaction(), commit_transaction(), commit_transaction_ssi()
3. ✅ MVCC-aware read: read_block_at_snapshot() checks MVCC store before device fallback
4. ✅ Write staging: stage_block_write() with transaction tracking
5. ✅ SSI support: record_read() for rw-antidependency tracking
6. ✅ Version GC: prune_mvcc_versions() for memory management
7. ✅ Comprehensive structured logging with tracing crate

## Files Modified:
- crates/ffs-core/Cargo.toml: Added parking_lot dependency
- crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs: Added MVCC integration (~200 lines)

## Logging Targets:
- ffs::mvcc for all MVCC operations
- txn_begin, txn_commit_start, txn_commit_success, txn_commit_conflict
- mvcc_read_start, mvcc_version_hit, mvcc_version_miss
- mvcc_write_stage, mvcc_read_set_add, mvcc_prune

## Verification:
- cargo fmt --check ✓
- cargo check --all-targets ✓
- cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings ✓
- cargo test --workspace ✓ (315+ tests passing)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-22w.3","depends_on_id":"bd-22w","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:51:10.376191065Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":33,"issue_id":"bd-22w.3","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"CRITICAL PATH: This bead is the keystone of the entire write path.  {"id":"bd-22w.4","title":"Implement MVCC read-set tracking with phantom detection","description":"# Implement MVCC read-set tracking with phantom detection

## GOAL
Track all blocks read during a transaction so SSI validation can detect read-write conflicts (phantoms) at commit time.

## BACKGROUND
True SSI requires detecting when a transaction T1 reads a block that was subsequently written by a committed transaction T2 (where T2 committed after T1's snapshot but before T1's commit). This is a "phantom" that indicates a potential serialization anomaly.

Currently, Transaction tracks reads but the conflict check only does FCW (first-committer-wins on the write set). Full SSI needs rw-antidependency tracking.

## DELIVERABLES
1. Read-set tracking: record (block_number, version_seen) for every block read
2. Phantom detection at commit: for each block in read-set, check if a newer version was committed since our snapshot
3. Conflict resolution: abort if phantom detected (serialization failure)
4. Integration with bd-38k (SSI write-skew detection)

## TESTS
1. Read-set correctly tracks all reads
2. Phantom detected when concurrent writer commits to read block
3. No false positives (no conflict when no phantom exists)
4. Performance: read-set tracking adds <1% overhead

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
Every SSI operation MUST emit structured logs with:
- `[SSI:ReadSet]` - read-set mutations: `trace!(target: "ffs::ssi", block_num=N, version_seen=V, txn_id=T, read_set_size=S, "read_set_add")`
- `[SSI:PhantomCheck]` - phantom detection: `debug!(target: "ffs::ssi", txn_id=T, block_num=N, snapshot_ver=V1, committed_ver=V2, is_phantom=bool, "phantom_check")`
- `[SSI:Conflict]` - conflict detection: `warn!(target: "ffs::ssi", txn_id=T, block_num=N, conflict_type="phantom|write_skew", action="abort", "ssi_conflict")`
- `[SSI:Commit]` - successful SSI validation: `info!(target: "ffs::ssi", txn_id=T, read_set_size=R, write_set_size=W, checks_performed=C, "ssi_commit_validated")`

Log levels:
- TRACE: every read-set addition
- DEBUG: every phantom check
- INFO: commit validation summary
- WARN: any conflict requiring abort

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Full SSI guarantees (not just FCW)
2. Correct abort on serialization failure
3. Minimal performance impact on read-heavy workloads
4. All logs parseable and queryable via grep/jq","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:54:21.784628601Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T04:31:18.020565224Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-22w.4","depends_on_id":"bd-22w","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:54:21.784628601Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-22w.4","depends_on_id":"bd-22w.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:54:38.686058872Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":43,"issue_id":"bd-22w.4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"SSI THEORY: Full SSI requires tracking rw-antidependencies. A rw-antidependency from T1 to T2 means: T1 read a block that T2 subsequently wrote. The dangerous pattern is a cycle: T1→T2→T1 (both have antidependencies on each other). In this case, one must abort to prevent a serialization anomaly. Cahill et al. 2008 (Making Snapshot Isolation Serializable) is the canonical reference.","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:57:58Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-22w.5","title":"Implement MVCC snapshot lifecycle with reference counting","description":"# Implement MVCC snapshot lifecycle with reference counting

## GOAL
Manage MVCC snapshot lifetimes so old versions can be safely garbage-collected when no active snapshot references them.

## BACKGROUND
Currently active_snapshots is a BTreeMap<CommitSeq, u32> with manual ref counting. This needs to be made robust and integrated with the FUSE request lifecycle:
- Each FUSE read operation gets a snapshot at its commit watermark
- The snapshot's ref count increments
- When the operation completes, the ref count decrements
- GC can prune versions older than the oldest active snapshot

## DELIVERABLES
1. SnapshotHandle RAII wrapper (decrements on drop)
2. Integration with ffs-fuse request lifecycle
3. Watermark advancement: when oldest snapshot drops, advance prune watermark
4. Integration with version GC (bd-22w.2 version chain compression)

## DESIGN
```rust
/// RAII handle that releases snapshot on drop
pub struct SnapshotHandle {
    snapshot: Snapshot,
    registry: Arc<SnapshotRegistry>,
}

impl Drop for SnapshotHandle {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        self.registry.release(self.snapshot);
    }
}

/// Thread-safe snapshot registry
pub struct SnapshotRegistry {
    active: RwLock<BTreeMap<CommitSeq, u32>>,
    watermark: AtomicU64,
}

impl SnapshotRegistry {
    pub fn acquire(&self) -> SnapshotHandle { ... }
    pub fn release(&self, snap: Snapshot) { ... }
    pub fn watermark(&self) -> CommitSeq { ... }
}
```

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit: Snapshot handle correctly increments ref count on create
2. Unit: Snapshot handle correctly decrements ref count on drop
3. Unit: GC respects oldest active snapshot (versions not pruned)
4. Unit: Watermark advances when oldest snapshot released
5. Unit: Multiple handles to same snapshot work correctly
6. Integration: No memory leak from forgotten snapshots (100k acquire/release)
7. Stress: Concurrent snapshot acquisition from 16 threads
8. Stress: Panic in FUSE handler still releases snapshot (Drop called)

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
### Lifecycle logging
- snapshot_acquire (trace): commit_seq, ref_count_after
- snapshot_release (trace): commit_seq, ref_count_after
- snapshot_final_release (debug): commit_seq (ref_count reached 0)

### Watermark logging
- watermark_advance (debug): old_watermark, new_watermark, snapshots_freed
- watermark_stall (info): current_watermark, oldest_active, stall_duration_secs (if > 60s)

### GC integration logging
- gc_eligible (trace): versions_count, watermark
- gc_blocked (debug): reason (active snapshots), oldest_active

### Error/warning logging
- orphan_snapshot (warn): commit_seq (snapshot never released, detected by timeout)
- ref_count_underflow (error): commit_seq (bug indicator)
- watermark_regression (error): old, new (should never happen)

### Metrics
- active_snapshots_count: Gauge
- oldest_active_snapshot_age_secs: Gauge
- snapshots_acquired_total: Counter
- snapshots_released_total: Counter

## EDGE CASES
1. Snapshot acquired, panic occurs before release → Drop still called
2. Snapshot acquired during GC → GC waits or skips
3. Very old snapshot held for long time → logged as stall
4. Rapid acquire/release (hot path) → no lock contention

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. No use-after-free of versioned blocks
2. Old versions pruned promptly when no longer needed
3. Thread-safe snapshot management
4. Panic-safe (RAII guarantees release)
5. Stalled snapshots detected and logged","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:54:21.893581588Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T08:56:15.150575342Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T08:56:15.150506113Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-22w.5","depends_on_id":"bd-22w","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:54:21.893581588Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-22w.5","depends_on_id":"bd-22w.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:54:38.884558176Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":42,"issue_id":"bd-22w.5","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"DESIGN RATIONALE: Snapshot lifecycle directly impacts GC safety. A SnapshotHandle that implements Drop ensures we never forget to release a snapshot, which would cause unbounded version chain growth (memory leak). The watermark advancement algorithm: when the oldest snapshot is released, scan active_snapshots for the new minimum—that becomes the new prune watermark.","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:57:58Z"}]}
